g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of the Current Program Status Register, a fundamental component of ARM processors.
The CPSR is a 32-bit register that holds condition flags, processor mode bits, and interrupt disable flags.
g.text: A 32-bit special-purpose register in ARM processors containing:

g.data-narration: The CPSR is divided into four main fields: Flags, Extension, Reserved, and Control fields spanning 32 bits.
The condition flags N, Z, C, V indicate the result of arithmetic operations. Q indicates DSP saturation.
ARM supports seven processor modes controlled by bits M4-M0 in the CPSR, each serving different purposes.
The I and F bits in CPSR disable IRQ and FIQ interrupts respectively, crucial for critical code sections.
ARM instructions can execute conditionally based on CPSR flags using two-letter suffix codes like EQ, NE, GT.
g.text: Most ARM instructions can be conditionally executed based on CPSR flags:

g.data-narration: MRS reads CPSR into a register; MSR writes to CPSR. Suffixes _c, _s, _x, _f access specific fields.
g.text: CPSR Access Instructions: MRS and MSR
Move Register from Status - Copy CPSR to R0
Move Status from Register - Copy R0 to CPSR

g.data-narration: Example demonstrates how flags change: MOVS sets N=1 (0xFFFFFFFF is negative), ADDS sets Z=1,C=1 (result is zero with carry).
This example finds the maximum of two numbers using CMP and conditional MOV instructions based on CPSR flags.
This example switches from User to Supervisor mode by manipulating CPSR mode bits using BIC and ORR operations.
Key takeaways: CPSR holds flags N/Z/C/V in bits 31-28, controls 7 processor modes, enables conditional execution, accessed via MRS/MSR.
g.text: EQ, NE, GT, LT, GE, CS, CC, MI, PL...
